**Tests.** `repl.test.ts`: a `!cmd` whose kill hook registers AFTER an EOF still gets SIGKILLed and quit completes (no hang). Verification: `tests/cli/repl` 120 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI: modal + interrupt events must bypass the tool min-display hold

Two findings on the `toolMinDisplayMs` hold queue (renderer.ts) — events queued behind a held `tool:end` broke synchronously-coupled REPL logic.

**Modal-open (security).** `modal-manager.ts` installs a modal's focus handler synchronously on `bus.emit(open(...))`, so the keyboard is routed to the modal the instant it's emitted. But the `*:ask` events weren't in `HOLD_BYPASS`, so a fast tool ending just before a permission/memory confirm queued the `*:ask` behind the 400ms hold: the prompt didn't render yet, but Enter / a hotkey was already consumed by the hidden handler — and permission prompts default to Yes, so an operator could approve a tool without ever seeing its modal. Fix: bypass all modal open (`permission:ask`, `trust:ask`, `shared-trust:ask`, `history-clear:ask`, `memory:{write,action,user-scope}:ask`) and lifecycle (`modal:answer/select/queue-depth`) events, so the modal renders in lockstep with focus activation.

**Interrupt escalation.** `triggerInterrupt()` reads `renderer.state().softInterrupted` to pick soft vs hard and emits `interrupt` to flip it. Behind the hold, the flip was queued, so a second Esc/Ctrl+C within the window re-read `false` → both presses took the soft branch, never the promised hard abort. Fix: bypass `interrupt` / `interrupt:exit-arm` / `interrupt:exit-cancel`. (Operator-`!cmd` interrupts already bypass this — they use a local latch, not the event.)

All bypassed events emit no permanent and touch only live-region/overlay state, so processing them out of order with the held `tool:end` is scrollback-safe. The `HOLD_BYPASS` comment now documents the three categories (keystroke/overlay, modal, interrupt).

**Tests.** `renderer.test.ts`: with a held `tool:end`, a `permission:ask` sets `state.modal` immediately (not queued); an `interrupt` flips `softInterrupted` immediately. Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 1026 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI: footer interrupt cue keys off `state.busy` (covers running `!cmd` / playbook)

**Finding.** The footer's interrupt cue (`esc to interrupt`) used `isTurnRunning` — turn-local activity (activeTools / thinking / streaming / awaiting). A running `!cmd` (e.g. `!sleep 5`) mirrors the REPL as busy and Ctrl+C/Esc kill it, but sets no turn fields, so the footer fell through to the idle `\+Enter newline` hint — making a hung operator command look non-interruptible even though the key path works.

**Fix.** The footer's `isRunning` now reads `state.busy` (the mirror of `isBusy()` = turn OR playbook OR operator `!cmd`), so the cue shows whenever any is interruptible. `busy` is set at startTurn (before `awaitingProvider`), so it still spans the whole turn — the awaiting-phase regression stays guarded. `isTurnRunning` had no remaining consumer once the bash gate moved to `state.busy`, so it's removed from `render/mode.ts`.

**Tests.** `footer.test.ts`: busy with no turn activity (a running `!cmd`) shows the interrupt cue. The existing running-turn cue tests now set `busy: true` (production-accurate — a turn mirrors busy). Verification: `tests/tui` 911 pass + `tests/cli/repl` 119 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean; `docs/TUI.md` module map updated. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI `!cmd`: replay an interrupt that beats the kill-hook registration

**Finding.** `runOperatorBash` sets `operatorBashRunning` synchronously but exposes the kill switch one microtask later (inside `Promise.resolve().then(() => execBash(…, onKillable))`). If Ctrl+C/Esc lands in that window — Enter + Ctrl+C in the same stdin chunk, or an async-registering test seam — `triggerInterrupt` sets the `operatorBashInterrupted` latch but `operatorBashKill` is still null, so the kill is a no-op. The `onKillable` callback then stored the kill function without checking the latch, dropping that first interrupt: the command ran until a second press or the timeout.

**Fix.** The `onKillable` callback now replays a pending interrupt — after storing the kill switch, `if (operatorBashInterrupted) kill('SIGINT')`. SIGINT is the first-tap signal; a later press still escalates to SIGKILL.

**Tests.** `repl.test.ts`: a seam that registers the hook only after the operator's Ctrl+C still terminates the command (replayed `killed:SIGINT`). Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 1029 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI: flatten nested (subagent) tool subjects too

**Finding.** The multi-line tool-subject flatten ran only on the top-level `tool_invoking` path; the subagent mirror (`subagent_progress` → `event.lastEvent.type === 'tool_invoking'`, emitting a parentId-tagged `tool:start`) forwarded `vocab.subject?.(inner.args)` raw. A subagent running a multi-line bash/heredoc command carried a literal `\n` into the nested card subject — the same one-element-spans-two-rows bug that leaks stale tool-card rows.

**Fix.** Extracted the collapse into a shared `flattenSubject` helper (harness-adapter.ts) and applied it on BOTH the top-level and the nested `tool:start` paths (deduping the regex). `\r`-free single-line subjects are untouched.

**Tests.** `harness-adapter.test.ts`: a subagent-mirrored multi-line bash command emits a `tool:start` with `parentId` set and a newline-free single-line subject. Verification: `tests/tui` 910 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] docs(tui): refresh TUI.md for the `!cmd` hardening + busy mirror

Brought `docs/TUI.md` up to date with the work since it was first written: the `!command` input-prefix section now covers the full hardening (busy-gating via `state.busy`, output sanitization — ANSI strip + CR→LF — byte cap + display cap, detached/interruptible lifecycle, shutdown tracking); the `LiveState` field list gains `busy`; the hold (§6.3) documents the three `HOLD_BYPASS` categories (keystroke/overlay, modal, interrupt) and why each would break if delayed; the module map gains `render/mode.ts`; event count bumped to ~58. Docs-only — no code. `docs/spec/` (PT-BR) untouched; the spec PR for the `!` escape + its security posture is still owed.

## [2026-06-03] TUI `!cmd`: normalize carriage returns in command output

**Finding.** `stripAnsi` (the intake sanitizer) keeps the whitespace controls TAB/LF/CR — but a bare `\r` survives, and the operator-bash card renders output rows verbatim under a 2-space frame margin. A `\r` returns the cursor to column 0, so a row from e.g. `!cat untrusted-file` (or progress-style output) overwrites the indentation / earlier content of that row — the exact spoofing the sanitization is meant to prevent.

**Fix.** A `sanitizeBashOutput` helper now wraps `stripAnsi` with `.replace(/\r\n?/g, '\n')` — `\r\n` and lone `\r` collapse to `\n`, so each would-be overwrite becomes a fresh (safe) row while the line structure is kept. Applied at the single intake point (both `operator-bash:done` emit paths). Only TAB/LF survive into the rendered output now.

**Tests.** `repl.test.ts`: output `KEEPME\rSPOOF` renders with both texts on separate rows and never as the column-0-overwrite adjacency. Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 1027 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI `!cmd`: ANSI-sanitize command output (terminal-hijack guard)

**Finding.** Operator command output was rendered VERBATIM to scrollback. A command like `!cat` on a file containing `ESC[2J` / OSC / hide-cursor would push raw control bytes to the terminal — clearing the screen, moving/hiding the cursor, or spoofing TUI chrome. The repo already treats this as terminal-state hijack (`src/sanitize/ansi.ts`, SECURITY_GUIDELINE §3.2).

**Fix.** `runOperatorBash` runs the output through `stripAnsi` before emitting `operator-bash:done` — sanitizing at the single intake point so every consumer is covered (the scrollback renderer AND the `--json` NDJSON forwarder), per the repo's strip-at-intake doctrine. `stripAnsi` removes ANSI escapes + C0/C1/DEL while keeping the safe whitespace controls (TAB/LF/CR), so multi-line text and the line split survive; command colors are dropped (safe over an SGR allowlist for now). The render layer keeps rendering verbatim — the output is now clean by the time it arrives (comment added in permanent.ts).

**Tests.** `repl.test.ts`: a command whose output contains `\x1b[2J…\x1b[?25l` lands with the visible text intact but the control sequences gone. Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 1024 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI `!cmd`: byte-cap output + track the command through shutdown

Two findings on the operator shell escape, both in `cli/repl.ts`.

**Byte-cap output (memory guard).** `new Response(proc.stdout).text()` buffered the WHOLE stream before the renderer's 200-line display cap applied — a flood (`!yes`, `!cat bigfile`) could exhaust memory long before the 120s timeout. The default executor now drains stdout chunk-by-chunk with a byte cap (`OPERATOR_BASH_MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES`, default 1 MiB): once the cap is crossed it SIGKILLs the process group (stops the producer) and appends a `[output capped …]` marker. Bounded to ~one pipe-buffer beyond the cap. Injectable via `operatorBashMaxOutputBytes`.

**Track through shutdown.** The operator promise was fire-and-forget, so EOF (Ctrl+D) / external SIGINT mid-`!cmd` closed the REPL while the detached group kept running (up to the timeout) and its completion tried to emit on a torn-down bus. `runOperatorBash` now stores the promise (`operatorBashPromise`); `shutdown` SIGKILLs the group (alongside the turn/playbook aborts) and awaits the promise (alongside `runningPromise` / `playbookPromise`), so the command dies with the REPL and its `operator-bash:done` lands before `renderer.close`. This also closes the earlier-noted Ctrl+D-orphan follow-up.

**Tests.** `repl.test.ts`: a `!yes` flood with a tiny byte cap lands its card fast (capped + killed, not the timeout); EOF while a `!cmd` hangs kills + awaits it and the card emits before teardown (`exit 130`). Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 1023 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI `!cmd`: bash-mode gate must match the submit gate (busy, not turn-running)

**Finding.** `isBashMode` gated on `!isTurnRunning(state)`, but `isTurnRunning` is a render-derived subset of the REPL's `isBusy()` — it only sees LiveState turn activity (activeTools / thinking / pendingAssistant / awaitingProvider) and can't see `playbookRunning` or `operatorBashRunning` (no LiveState reflection). So while another `!cmd` ran, or in a playbook gap, typing a leading `!` flipped the input/rules/footer to yellow shell mode even though Enter refuses the command as busy. The visual gate didn't match the submit gate.

**Fix.** Mirror `isBusy()` into renderer state. New `busy:change` UIEvent → `LiveState.busy` (reducer). A deduped `syncBusy()` helper in the REPL emits it after every mutation of `running` / `playbookRunning` / `operatorBashRunning` (7 sites). `isBashMode` now gates on `!state.busy` — the exact predicate the submit path uses — so the shell visuals appear only when a `!` would actually run. `state.busy` isn't touched by session boundaries (it tracks the REPL, not a turn). The footer's interrupt cue keeps using `isTurnRunning` (its own concern); only the bash gate moved to `busy`.

**Tests.** `state.test.ts`: `busy:change` sets/clears `state.busy`. `footer.test.ts` / `compose.test.ts`: shell visuals suppressed when `busy` with NO turn activity (the playbook / running-`!cmd` case `isTurnRunning` missed). `repl.test.ts`: typing `!` while a first `!cmd` hangs shows no shell indicator (end-to-end busy:change). Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 1021 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI `!cmd` follow-up: interruptible, ordered output, single-line card

Three review findings on the shipped operator shell escape (commit above), all fixed in `cli/repl.ts` + `render/permanent.ts`:

**Interruptible (`cli/repl.ts`).** `operatorBashRunning` is in `isBusy()`, so Ctrl+C/Esc already routed to `triggerInterrupt()` — but that only aborted the turn/playbook controllers and had no handle on the spawned shell, so the interrupt was cosmetic and the REPL stayed blocked until the 120s timeout. The default `execBash` now hands its process-group kill switch up via an `onKillable` callback; `runOperatorBash` stores it and `triggerInterrupt` kills the command's group when one is running (SIGINT on the first tap, SIGKILL on a repeat if it ignores SIGINT — mirroring the turn soft/hard ladder), returning before the turn-level `interrupt` event (no turn to interrupt; emitting it would leak `softInterrupted` into the next turn). The Esc gate (`running`) widened to `running || operatorBashRunning`; Ctrl+C already covered via `isBusy`.

**stdout/stderr order (`cli/repl.ts`).** Reading the two pipes independently and concatenating `out + err` shoved every diagnostic after all normal output, misrepresenting a compiler/test transcript. The default `execBash` now prefixes the script with `exec 2>&1` (redirects the shell's fd 2 onto fd 1 for the whole command) and reads only stdout, so stderr interleaves in emission order on one pipe.

**Single-line card (`render/permanent.ts`).** A multi-line command (the editor accepts `\`+Enter / Shift+Enter) interpolated into the `! <command>` head spilled a raw `\n` onto an unprefixed scrollback row and pushed the `exit N` / `[dur]` metrics below the command text. The head now flattens the command's newlines to spaces for display; it still RAN with the real newlines.

**Tests.** `repl.test.ts`: Ctrl+C kills a running `!cmd` via SIGINT (no timeout wait); a SIGINT-ignoring command is SIGKILLed on the second tap; real-spawn `printf OUTA; printf ERRB 1>&2; printf OUTC` preserves OUTA→ERRB→OUTC order. `permanent.test.ts`: a multi-line command renders as one flattened head row. Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 1018 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI: operator shell escape — `!cmd` runs a command from the input

**Goal.** Operator request: type `!` in the input to run a shell command. `!` flips the prompt `> ` → `! ` and paints the line yellow; the output lands in scrollback. Operator chose the **direct shell** model (vs gating through the agent's permission engine / sandbox): the engine gates the agent, not the human at the keyboard, so `!` is a real shell escape.

**Input UX + mode predicate (`render/input.ts`, `render/mode.ts`, `render/footer.ts`).** Bash mode flips the prompt `> ` → `! ` (the leading `!` is consumed as the glyph, not shown as content), paints the whole input row + both rules yellow (`warn`, SGR 33), and replaces the footer cues with a single `! for shell mode`. One predicate — `isBashMode(state)` in `render/mode.ts` — is the single source of truth for every render site (input, rules, `composeCursor` caret strip, footer); they MUST agree or they desync, so it's centralized rather than re-derived in four places. It's **idle-gated** (`!isTurnRunning(state)`): a `!` typed mid-turn would be refused on submit anyway, so flipping to shell visuals then would advertise a dead mode AND shadow the load-bearing `esc to interrupt` footer cue — instead the buffer stays a normal gray draft and flips to bash mode the moment the turn ends. `composeCursor` mirrors the strip (`value.slice(1)`, cursor −1) so the caret stays aligned. `renderInput` receives `bash` as a flag (composeLive decides it, since the idle gate needs the full state) rather than re-detecting.

**Dispatch + exec (`cli/repl.ts`).** New `!`-prefix branch in the Enter handler, ranked before the inbox/turn split: it runs as the operator's own shell, not the agent (no `user:submit` echo, no inbox queueing). The default `execBash` spawns `bash -c` **`detached`** in the REPL cwd with the operator's full env; on timeout it SIGKILLs the whole **process group** (`process.kill(-pid, …)`, the bg-manager convention) — a bare `proc.kill()` would leave pipeline children / SIGTERM-ignorers holding the stdout pipe open, stalling the stream read forever and wedging `operatorBashRunning` (and thus `isBusy`). `runOperatorBash` defers the call through `Promise.resolve().then(...)` so a synchronously-throwing `execBash` becomes a caught rejection, not an escaped throw that leaves the flag stuck. Serialized via `isBusy()` (now also covers `operatorBashRunning`): refused with a warn while a turn / playbook / another `!` is in flight (command left in the buffer to re-run). Bare `!` clears. Exec + timeout are injectable (`RunReplOptions.execBash` / `operatorBashTimeoutMs`) for tests.

**Scrollback (`state.ts` + `render/permanent.ts`).** New `operator-bash:done` UIEvent → reducer → `operator-bash` PermanentItem → `formatPermanent` card: head `! <command>` (dim) + a warn `exit N` when it failed + secondary `[dur]`, output shown VERBATIM below (operator's own shell — not recolored), capped at 200 lines with a `… +N more lines` tail. Not injected into the agent's context (scrollback only — matches the request).

**Review fixes (high-effort code-review before commit).** Four findings, all fixed: (1) the timeout's `proc.kill()` couldn't terminate a pipe-holding / SIGTERM-ignoring command → REPL could wedge forever — fixed with `detached` + process-group SIGKILL; (2) the footer (and rules / input) showed shell mode while a turn ran, hiding the interrupt cue and advertising a refused mode — fixed by idle-gating `isBashMode`; (3) a synchronously-throwing `execBash` could leave `operatorBashRunning` stuck — fixed with the deferred-call wrapper; (4) the bash-mode predicate was duplicated across four render sites — centralized in `render/mode.ts`.

**Tests.** `input.test.ts`: bash-mode prompt + yellow line, continuation rows, color=none, dim-wins-over-bash, `!`-without-flag stays plain. `compose.test.ts`: caret skips the `!` glyph; rules yellow when idle, suppressed mid-turn. `permanent.test.ts`: success card, empty-output head-only, warn `exit N`, output cap. `footer.test.ts`: shell indicator when idle, suppressed mid-turn (interrupt cue kept), suppressed under reverse-search. `state.test.ts`: `operator-bash:done` → permanent + batch-flush ordering. `repl.test.ts`: `!cmd` runs (`!` stripped, output in scrollback), bare `!` no-op, sync-throw `execBash` doesn't wedge, hung command killed by the timeout (real spawn). Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 1014 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. `docs/TUI.md` updated (input-prefix modes + the new PermanentItem kind). No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec follow-up.** A new REPL surface — `docs/spec/UI.md` (§5 input model) doesn't describe an operator shell escape, and SECURITY_GUIDELINE doesn't cover operator-direct (unsandboxed) execution. Owes a spec amendment documenting the `!` escape + its security posture (operator-initiated, ungated by design). Also reuses `warn` (yellow) for non-warning content, same §6.4 palette-amendment debt as the `accent` slash highlight.

## [2026-06-03] TUI: blue slash-command token in the input

**Goal.** Operator request — when the input buffer is a `/command`, color it so it reads as command mode, not a message being composed.

**Change.** `render/input.ts` paints the leading command token (slash + word, up to the first whitespace) in `accent` (SGR 94, blue) when the buffer is a single line starting with `/`. Args after the token keep the normal tone (standard shell-style: highlight the command name, not its arguments). The token always lives in chunk 0; if a long command wraps, only the in-chunk slice of the token is colored. Skipped when `dimmed` (reverse-search owns the palette) and when the buffer spans lines (slash commands are single-line). `paint` no-ops under `color='none'`, and the SGR is zero-width so `composeCursor` / `truncateToWidth` are unaffected (verified — column math reads `input.value`, not the painted string).

**Tests.** `input.test.ts`: token painted accent with args plain (`> \x1b[94m/effort\x1b[0m high`); bare `/` turns blue immediately; non-slash input uncolored; no-op under `color='none'`; dimmed slash line dims (SGR 2) rather than coloring. Verification: `tests/tui` 887 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec follow-up.** This is a new use of `accent` for *content* — `term.ts` reserves accent/accentDark for layout chrome ("NOT for content") pending a `UI.md §6.4` palette amendment. Consistent with how accent/accentDark already shipped as operator-authorized deviations; the amendment now owes this case too (UX-before-spec convention).

## [2026-06-03] TUI: wcwidth-aware input wrapping + cursor column (close the last width-vs-reality gap)

**Motivation.** A width-vs-reality audit of the live-region cursor/erase math found one remaining structural desync, explicitly deferred in `wrap.ts` ("solved by a wcwidth-aware chunker (deferred)"): the input editor wrapped by UTF-16 code units, not visual columns. A buffer line of wide glyphs (CJK / emoji = 2 cols) packed `innerWidth` *code units* per chunk, so the rendered row was ~2× the terminal width; the terminal soft-wrapped it onto a second visual row while `renderInput` + `composeCursor` counted it as one → `liveHeight` / `cursorRow` undercount → stale rows leak into scrollback (same class as the multi-line-subject bug, but input-only). A paired cosmetic gap: `composeCursor` computed the caret column from the code-unit offset, so the cursor sat one column left per wide glyph before it.

**Fix.** `wrapInputLine` (`wrap.ts`) now accumulates `visualWidth` per codepoint up to `innerWidth` instead of counting code units; it still iterates per-codepoint (1-or-2 code-unit step) so surrogate pairs are never split, and still returns code-unit `[start,end)` ranges so `renderInput` and `composeCursor` consume one shared list. A lone glyph wider than the budget gets its own over-budget chunk (forward-progress guarantee). `composeCursor` (`compose.ts`) computes the sub-row column as `prefixWidth + visualWidth(line.slice(chunkStart, cursorOffset))`. Both row count (chunk count) and column are now visual-width-correct end to end; each emitted input row fits within `caps.cols`, so the terminal never soft-wraps it.

**Audit result (rest is clean).** All non-input live content already routes through `truncateToWidth` (visual-width-aware), so it never overflows → no wrap-induced ghost rows; embedded newlines anywhere in the live region are split by `composeRows` (prior fix); scrollback isn't truncated but sits above the live region and is never erased. The input editor was the last place measuring by code units.

**Tests.** `wrap.test.ts`: wraps by visual width (`'中'×6` at width 8 → 4 per row, not 8 code units), exact-fit wide chars, lone over-budget glyph, surrogate-pair integrity (comments updated — the property holds via codepoint-stepping, not the old pull-back). `compose.test.ts`: cursor column counts visual width of wide glyphs before the caret. Existing ASCII/surrogate cases unchanged (😀 is 2 units = 2 cols, so boundaries coincide). Verification: `tests/tui` 882 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-03] TUI fix: multi-line tool subject leaked a stale card into scrollback

**Symptom.** A live "Awaiting approval" card for a multi-line bash command (a `for` loop / heredoc) froze into scrollback mid-turn instead of being erased when the tool resolved. Surfaced once the min-display hold kept awaiting cards on screen longer, but it's a pre-existing invariant break.

**Root cause.** The live region is an array where each element is contracted to be ONE terminal row — `liveHeight`, `cursorRow`, the erase walk-back (`cursorUp(cursorRow)` + `clearDown`), and the differential diff all count array elements as rows. A tool `subject` carries the model's raw args verbatim (`tool-vocab` `bash.subject = str(a.command)`), so a multi-line command put a literal `\n` inside one element. That element renders as TWO terminal rows but counts as one → `cursorRow` undercounts → on the next permanent emit the erase walks up too few rows → `clearDown` starts below the top of the live region → the top rows (the awaiting card) survive as scrollback.

**Fix (two layers).** (1) Source: `harness-adapter.ts` flattens any subject containing `\n` to a single line (`/\s*\n\s*/g → ' '`, trimmed) — multi-line commands become one clean `└─ a && b ; c` row; single-line subjects (paths, patterns) untouched, intra-line spacing preserved. (2) Invariant guard: `renderer.ts` `composeRows()` wraps every `composeLive` call with `.flatMap(l => l.split('\n'))`, so ANY future newline source (an error summary, a tool preview) is split into real rows before counting — the single chokepoint that keeps element-count == row-count. Both call sites (`writeTransition`, `redraw`, `screen:clear`) route through it.

**Tests.** `harness-adapter.test.ts`: multi-line bash command → newline-free single-line subject; single-line subject untouched (no whitespace mangling). The guard is a one-line defensive split exercised by every renderer test on each draw (the leak itself isn't unit-observable — a string sink can't model the terminal's `\n`-row-wrap, which is the whole discrepancy). Verification: `tests/tui` 877 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-02] TUI: minimum on-screen time for tool cards (fast tools no longer flash-and-vanish)

**Motivation.** Operator report: live tool cards (read / write / quick bash) "just flash and disappear" — `tem como deixar sempre visível enquanto estiver executando?`. Root cause traced through the renderer: the frame scheduler (`term.ts`) coalesces redraws to ~one per 33ms frame, and a fast tool emits `tool:start` → `tool:execution-started` → `tool:end` inside a single frame budget. By the time the coalesced frame draws, the `tool:end` reducer has already deleted the tool from `activeTools`, so `composeLive` iterates an empty map and the card never paints. The scrollback line is also deferred (successful tools buffer into `pendingToolEndBatch`, flushed only on the next permanent-emitting event), so the operation leaves no trace until later.

**Change.** New `RendererOptions.toolMinDisplayMs` (renderer.ts). When > 0, the renderer HOLDS each `tool:end` (and the harness events queued behind it) until the card has been on screen at least that long, then resumes. While held, the tool is still in `activeTools`, so the live region keeps showing and animating the card (the heartbeat stays armed). Implemented as a FIFO event queue + a one-shot hold timer (reusing `schedulerOptions` timer hooks + `now` for deterministic tests); the existing `handleEvent` body became `processEvent`, and a thin `handleEvent` gates entry. `close()` drains any held events and flushes the pending tool-end batch so teardown never drops a finalization.

**Keystroke bypass.** Holding the queue would lag typing (the operator can type the next message while tools run). A `HOLD_BYPASS` set (`input:update`, `slash:update`, `reverse-search:update/close`) routes keystroke / overlay events straight to `processEvent` — they touch only input/overlay state, emit no permanent, and commute with the held tool lifecycle, so applying them out of band is safe. The bypass list errs on the side of omission: a missing entry costs at most a `toolMinDisplayMs` delay, never a scrollback-reordering bug.

**Tradeoff (accepted).** The hold delays subsequent harness events (including the assistant's follow-up text and further tools) by up to `toolMinDisplayMs` per held fast tool — the turn renders as a watchable replay slightly behind real execution. That pacing IS the requested behavior. Only FAST tools are held; a tool already visible ≥ the threshold has `remaining <= 0` and passes through untouched.

**Wiring.** `toolMinDisplayMs` defaults to 0 (off) in both `createRenderer` and `runRepl`, so the renderer/REPL test-suites drive tool flows synchronously with zero added latency. The production entrypoint (`cli/index.ts`) passes **400ms**. One REPL test (`Phase 3: tool card from a resumed session renders into scrollback`) asserts post-tool assistant text synchronously and would have hung on a real 400ms timer — the 0 default keeps it (and all other harness tests) unaffected.

**Scrollback persistence + grouping (follow-up — live batch preview).** Two related reports: (a) a fast `done` tool mid-turn left no scrollback line until a far-off flush trigger, so it flashed then vanished; (b) a sequential run of bash produced N separate `● Executed` blocks instead of one group. Both stem from `pendingToolEndBatch`: consecutive same-name tools coalesce into a single `● Executed N commands` summary (`batchHeadlineVerb`), but the batch is INVISIBLE while accumulating and only settles into scrollback on a later trigger (different-name tool / assistant text / session:end). An interim fix (flush each `tool:end` immediately in hold mode) fixed the vanish but defeated grouping. Final fix renders the accumulating batch LIVE: `compose.ts` formats the would-be flush items (`flushPendingToolEndBatch(state).permanent`, pure — `.permanent` read, cleared state discarded) through the SAME `formatPermanent` the scrollback path uses, placed above the running tool cards. Result: completed tools stay visible AND grouped while they accumulate, and when the batch settles the block moves from live region to scrollback with no visual jump (same formatter → byte-identical). Coalescing is restored (interim per-tool flush reverted); the "Read 3 files" / "Executed N commands" fold works in hold mode again. Sub-threshold (single tool) previews as one `tool-end` line, matching what a sub-threshold flush emits.

**Tests.** `renderer.test.ts` `tool min-display hold` block (injected clock + scheduler timers): holds a fast `tool:end` then releases it; keystrokes bypass the hold; default 0 removes the card immediately; a released tool stays visible via the live batch preview with no session:end (the no-vanish guarantee); `close()` flushes a held tool so its chip survives. `compose.test.ts`: live batch preview renders the grouped `Executed N commands` block (≥ threshold) and a single `tool-end` line (sub-threshold), matching the scrollback formatter. Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli/repl` 979 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Open tradeoff (pacing).** The 400ms per-tool hold and the live grouping are independent. With both on, a run of N fast tools paces the running card N×400ms behind real execution while the group accumulates — watchable but laggy for long bash runs. The live batch preview alone already prevents the vanish, so the hold could be dropped for snappier grouped output; left in for now (operator approved the hold earlier) — revisit if the pacing reads as sluggish in `bun run dev`.

## [2026-06-02] TUI: tool-phase spinner chip — keep a live verb during tool execution

**Motivation.** The top chip slot (`compose.ts`) showed a rotating spinner verb only during the thinking (`pickCognitiveVerb`) and text-streaming (`pickOutputVerb`) phases. During the tool-execution phase — model idle, harness running the calls it emitted — `thinking`, `pendingAssistant` and `awaitingProvider` are all null, so the slot went blank and only the tool cards animated beneath it. The verb the operator had been tracking vanished mid-turn; the ask was to keep an indicator live for the whole interaction.

**Change.** Third verb pool + chip, mirroring the existing two. `spinner-verbs.ts`: new `TOOL_VERBS` (the "agent infrastructure" cluster the comments already reserved — Orchestrating/Dispatching/Sequencing/Coordinating/Consolidating), `TOOL_SALT`, `pickToolVerb`, `TOOL_VERB_POOL` seam. New `render/tool-phase-chip.ts`: `▸ Orchestrating…` — spinner + shimmer verb, deliberately NO timer/token counter (each tool card owns its own `[Xs]`; a turn-level timer would duplicate them or jump as tools complete). `compose.ts`: new chip-slot branch (`activeTools.size > 0`, ranked after thinking/assistant, before `awaitingProvider`) so it fills only when the model is idle and tools run, staying mutually exclusive with the other chips.

**Layout (operator request).** Inverted the old "chip as parent above its tool calls" arrangement: the turn-phase chip-slot now renders at the BOTTOM of the live region (directly above the input block) and the tool cards stack ABOVE it. The operator's eye rests near the typing zone, so the always-present status pins there while the volatile tool stack (read/write/bash…) grows upward. Cursor math untouched — the slot stays in the upper region (above the input rule), so `composeCursor` / `trailingBelowInput` are unaffected. The chip is effectively always-on during a turn (Awaiting → Thinking → Generating → Orchestrating cover the phases); between turns all four states are null and the slot collapses.

**Stable seed.** Added `currentTurnId: string | null` to `LiveState`, set by whichever of `thinking:start` / `assistant:start` opens the turn and held through the tool phase (both `thinking` and `pendingAssistant` clear before tools run, so neither can seed a chip there). The tool-phase verb hashes off the turn id — stable across individual tool start/end churn (seeded off the turn, not any tool id), independent of the cognitive/output verbs (per-pool salt). Cleared at session boundaries (same rationale as `awaitingProvider`). Defensive fallback seed `'tools'` when the id is null.

**Tests.** `spinner-verbs.test.ts`: pool composition pinned, three-way no-overlap, `pickToolVerb` determinism/distribution, tool↔cognitive/output cross-pool independence. New `tool-phase-chip.test.ts`: verb-from-pool, stability across ticks, null-seed fallback, no-timer/no-counter, ASCII. `compose.test.ts`: tool-phase chip pinned below the tool cards; assistant chip wins (no tool-phase chip) while text streams; layout tests updated for the new top→bottom order (TodoList → tool cards → pinned chip). Verification: `tests/tui` 866 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec follow-up.** Deferred per the UX-before-spec convention — `UI.md` chip-slot / spinner-verb prose to be reconciled once the tool-phase verb is validated in `bun run dev`.

## [2026-06-02] memory prompt: make `[seed]` entries operative (close the seed-incentive gap)

**Motivation.** An audit of the system-prompt assembly (`src/cli/memory-prompt.ts`) found the eager `# Memory` section framed every entry — including vendor `[seed]` entries — as reference the model "can use", with save-guidance + a verify-before-act caution, but NO incentive to apply them. Seeds are behavioral meta-rules meant to be followed, yet (a) their bodies are lazy (only the one-line hook is eager; the Why/How-to-apply loads only via `memory_read`) and (b) the `[seed]` marker's only stated job was authority transparency (`memory-prompt.ts:317`). Net: a behavioral seed could sit in the index unread and unapplied. (Note: the root premise "measure twice, cut once" is exempt — it's hard-coded in `IDENTITY_PROMPT`, the always-first frame — but the other 10 seeds depend on being read.)

**Change.** Add one directive paragraph to `MEMORY_SECTION_HEADER`: entries tagged `[seed]` are vendor-curated default disciplines; treat them as operative — when a seed's hook bears on the action at hand, `memory_read` its body and apply it — defaults an operator memory or explicit instruction can override. This keeps the content-lazy architecture (spec §4.1 "index eager, content lazy") and adds the missing "operative, consult them" framing + the precedence rule, at ~1 paragraph of prompt cost.

**Rejected: eager-load seed bodies.** Inlining every seed body was rejected — it contradicts §4.1 and the §5.7.7 cap rationale ("each seed costs context every session forever"), multiplying the per-session seed cost ~25× (11 bodies × ~25 lines). Lazy-with-a-nudge preserves the budget.

**Tests.** `memory-prompt.test.ts`: + a positive assertion that the directive renders; the "operator eclipses seed → no marker" assertion tightened from the bare `[seed]` token to the entry-specific marker (the header now mentions `[seed]` in prose). `bootstrap.test.ts`: the header-only assertion likewise matches the rendered entry marker (` [seed] —`) instead of the bare token. Verification: `memory-prompt` + `bootstrap` + `memory` + `provenance-trail` + `lifecycle` 174 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec follow-up.** `[seed]` semantics live in MEMORY §5.7.3 (transparency marker) + §4.1 (index-eager) + the §1.2 prompt skeleton; documenting the "operative meta-behavior" framing there is a deferred reconciliation (prose iterated in code first, per the UX-before-spec convention).

## [2026-06-02] seeds: translate the vendor pack to English (src/ language policy)

**Goal.** The 11 vendor seeds (`src/cli/init-seeds/*.md`) shipped in PT-BR, which violates the project language policy — everything under `src/` is English; only `docs/spec/` stays PT-BR. Rewrite all seed bodies + frontmatter descriptions in English. The `docs/spec/MEMORY.md §5.7.8` catalog table stays PT-BR (spec is the architect's PT-BR material, not translated without an explicit request).

**Scope.** All 11 `.md` bodies + `description` fields, kept semantically identical (same rules, same **Why** / **How to apply** structure, ≤30-line cap). Tool/command identifiers (`Edit`, `Write`, `Grep`, `--no-verify`, git invocations) and the `name` slugs were already English — unchanged. `seed_version` stays `"1.0"`: pre-release, no installed base to migrate, and the catalog test pins `"1.0"`.

**Sync + safety.** Each `index.ts` `CANONICAL_SEEDS` entry's `description` updated to byte-match its `.md` frontmatter (the catalog test asserts equality). Descriptions kept colon-free (frontmatter-safe) and apostrophe-free in `no-auto-commit` (single-quoted JS literal). Verified no other coupling first: no test asserts PT-BR seed body text, none imports the bodies — the only consumers read `CANONICAL_SEEDS.length`.

**Tests.** No test changes needed — `tests/cli/init-seeds.test.ts` re-validates description↔frontmatter equality + body ≤30 over the new English content. Verification: `tests/cli/init-seeds` + `seeds-installer` + `memory` + `slash/memory` 290 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-02] seed `measure-twice-cut-once` — umbrella due-diligence seed (vendor pack 10→11)

**Goal.** Ship the project's root premise ("measure twice, cut once" / due-diligence) as a first-class vendor seed. Operator chose to raise the §5.7.7 hard cap 10→11 (over merging into an existing seed) so the umbrella principle ships as its own behavioral seed rather than staying implicit across the 10 specifics.

**Why a seed, not a doctrinal echo.** The existing 10 seeds are each *instances* of measure-twice — `confirm-blast-radius` (map blast radius before the cut), `safe-edit-discipline` (read before edit), `no-fabrication` (verify before asserting), `failure-root-cause` (diagnose before fixing). What none states is the *general* test for ANY persistent side effect, plus the "declare the unmeasured" disclosure (`not_checked` / `assumptions` / `confidence`) and the reversible-with-fallback rule. The seed is written as that actionable general test — deliberately NOT a restatement of `AGENTIC_CLI §1` (which §5.7.10 flags as an anti-pattern: "seed que duplica AGENTIC_CLI.md").

**Cap bump (spec change, operator-authorized).** `docs/spec/MEMORY.md`: §5.7.7 hard cap 10→11; §5.7.10 anti-pattern row "Pack default > 10" → "> 11"; §5.7.8 catalog table gains the `measure-twice-cut-once` lead/umbrella row + a "**Novo:**" note recording the 10→11 rationale. The cap's reasoning (each seed costs context every session) still holds at 11; the root premise earns the marginal slot.

**Code.** New `src/cli/init-seeds/measure-twice-cut-once.md` (frontmatter `source: seed` / `vendor` / `"1.0"` / `trusted`; body within the 30-line cap; description uses an em-dash, not `:`, to stay frontmatter-safe). Registered in `src/cli/init-seeds/index.ts` (import + entry, alphabetical between `git-first-orientation` and `no-auto-commit`); cap comments bumped 10→11 / 11→12. `docs/MEMORY.md` §7.5 + the layout illustration refreshed 10→11 (incl. the derived `N more` / `N wrote` examples); the milestone-history note at the file's tail left as the point-in-time record it is.

**Tests.** `tests/cli/init-seeds.test.ts`: length assertions + titles 10→11. The catalog's existing gates (seed cross-field frontmatter, body ≤30, name/description match the index entry, unique + alphabetically-ordered filenames) cover the new seed unchanged. No other consumer hardcoded the count — `seeds-installer` / `memory` / `slash/memory` tests all read `CANONICAL_SEEDS.length`. Verification: `tests/cli/init-seeds` 8 pass; `tests/memory/seeds-installer` + `tests/cli/memory` + `tests/cli/slash/memory` 282 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

## [2026-06-01] effort — agnostic reasoning + operational effort level (`/effort`)

**Goal.** A single `effort` knob (`low | medium | high | max`) that drives TWO axes at once, per the operator design tenet "provider effort controls the model's internal depth; harness effort controls the amount of operational work." Session-scoped (in memory, next-turn — like `/model`/`/budget`); no config/DB persistence (operator decided in-memory only). Provider-agnostic by construction: one abstraction, a small per-adapter translation, so adding a provider is ~4 lines, not a special case.

**Design.**
- **Abstraction (single vocabulary).** `ProviderEffort = low|medium|high|max` (`src/providers/types.ts`), carried on `GenerateRequest.effort`. `ForjaEffort` (`src/harness/effort.ts`) = same levels, with `EFFORT_PROFILES` mapping each level to `{ providerEffort, maxSteps, maxConcurrentSubagents, maxToolErrors }` — projecting onto fields `RunBudget` ALREADY owns (no parallel budget). Verification (tests/self-review) deliberately is NOT a profile field — that's a playbook/hook concern (PostToolUse gate / ORCHESTRATION §6 self-critique), not inline scheduler logic.
- **Per-adapter translation** (`src/providers/effort.ts` holds the canonical ladder + OpenAI map): Anthropic → `output_config.effort` (low/med/high/max **native**, 1:1); OpenAI → `reasoning.effort` (max→xhigh); Gemini 2.5 → numeric `thinkingConfig.thinkingBudget` from the canonical ladder (clamped < max_tokens); a future Gemini 3+ slots `thinkingLevel` into the same function.
- **Anthropic adaptive-thinking migration (fixes a live bug).** The adapter sent `thinking:{type:'enabled',budget_tokens}` unconditionally — but per the Messages API (verified 2026-06-01) that is **rejected with HTTP 400 on Opus 4.7** (registered today) and Opus 4.8, where `adaptive` is the only thinking mode; deprecated on Sonnet 4.6. So any playbook with `sampling.thinking_budget` (`gap-audit`, `perf-investigate`, `threat-model`, `challenge-assumptions`) currently 400s on Opus 4.7. New `supports_adaptive_thinking` capability gates: when true → `thinking:{type:'adaptive'}` (budget dropped; the `>= max_tokens` cross-check is skipped); else → legacy `enabled+budget_tokens`.
- **Surface.** `/effort [low|medium|high|max]` mutates `baseConfig.effort` + writes the profile's caps into `baseConfig.budget` in place (next-turn, with the running-turn cue); bare `/effort` shows the level + resolved caps + per-provider mapping.

**Spec.** Cross-cutting (TOKEN_TUNING §4 reasoning effort, ORCHESTRATION/STATE_MACHINE steps, PLAYBOOKS verification). Per operator decision this lands as **code + BACKLOG only**; reconciling `docs/spec/` (incl. renaming §4.1's `off/conservative/balanced/maximum` ladder to the `low/medium/high/max` effort vocabulary and documenting `output_config.effort` + adaptive thinking) is an explicit follow-up, NOT done here.

**Follow-ups.** Remaining: (1) Register Opus 4.8 in `ANTHROPIC_CAPS` (separate from this feature). (2) Gemini 3+ `thinkingLevel` mapping when such a model is registered. (3) Spec reconciliation above. — Done in review round 2 (see below): (4) subagent effort inheritance, (5) two-axis precedence resolver, (6) Gemini budget floor, (7) `withRunningCue` extraction.

**Tests.** 28 new across 4 files: `tests/harness/effort.test.ts` (profiles monotonic, providerEffort 1:1, `effortBudgetPatch` assignable to `Partial<RunBudget>`), `tests/providers/effort.test.ts` (OpenAI map max→xhigh, ladder monotonic + ≤24_576, clamp < max_tokens + undefined on no headroom), `tests/providers/anthropic-effort.test.ts` (`anthropicThinkingParam` adaptive-vs-legacy matrix + fake-client integration: opus-4-7 → `output_config.effort`+`thinking:adaptive` w/ NO budget_tokens, haiku-4-5 → `enabled`+budget_tokens, none → neither), `/effort` block in `tests/cli/slash/commands.test.ts` (set/show/idempotent/unknown/too-many-args/case-insensitive/budget projection preserves unrelated caps/running cue). Updated existing: 3 in `anthropic.test.ts` (cross-check tests retargeted `sonnet-4-6`→`haiku-4-5` since adaptive now skips the legacy `budget_tokens` check) + builtin count 16→17 / 19→20 in `dispatch.test.ts`. Verification: `tests/providers` + `tests/harness` **491 pass / 0 fail**; `tests/cli/slash` **607 pass / 0 fail**; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean.

**Review fixes (code-review, high — 2026-06-02).** A multi-angle review caught real bugs in the first cut (the OpenAI path I'd reported as done was broken; root cause was a missing capability gate). Fixed:
- **OpenAI wrong field shape (blocker):** adapter set nested `params.reasoning = { effort }` (Responses-API shape) — Chat Completions takes the FLAT `reasoning_effort`. Would 400 every OpenAI turn (or silently no-op on lenient proxies). `tsc` couldn't catch it (`params: Record<string,unknown>` + cast) and the original tests only asserted the map table, never the live write. Fixed to `reasoning_effort`; extracted pure `openaiReasoningParam(req, caps)` so the live decision is unit-tested.
- **Missing capability gate (root cause):** effort was emitted to every model gated only on `req.effort !== undefined`. Added `ProviderCapabilities.supports_reasoning_effort`; each adapter now emits its effort surface only when true. Set true on `opus-4-7`/`sonnet-4-6` (Anthropic) and the Gemini 2.5 base; left OFF on `gpt-4o`/`gpt-4o-mini` (non-reasoning → would 400) and `haiku-4-5` (uncertain `output_config.effort` support). This also neutralizes the `max`→`xhigh` latent 400 (never emitted without a flagged model).
- **disable-via-zero respected:** an explicit `thinking_budget: 0` now wins over a session `effort` on both Anthropic (`anthropicThinkingParam` early-return) and Gemini (`googleThinkingBudget` precedence) — effort no longer resurrects thinking a playbook turned off.
- **Packaging regression reverted (out-of-scope drift):** `@anthropic-ai/sdk` pin restored `^`→`~0.91.1` and the stray npm `package-lock.json` (a Bun project) removed.
- Extracted `googleThinkingBudget(req, caps)` too (symmetry + testability).

Review tests: +disable-via-zero case in `anthropic-effort.test.ts`; haiku integration test flipped to assert `output_config` NOT emitted; new `tests/providers/openai-effort.test.ts` (flat `reasoning_effort`, gate, max→xhigh) and `tests/providers/google-effort.test.ts` (ladder map, disable-zero, capability gate, precedence). Post-fix verification: `tests/providers`+`tests/harness`+`tests/cli/slash` **1108 pass / 0 fail**; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Review follow-ups round 2 (code-review, high — 2026-06-02).** Cleared findings (5)/(8)/(9)/(10) from the round-1 review:
- **(9) Two-axis precedence → resolver.** `/effort` no longer mutates `baseConfig.budget` in place. It records the level only; `effectiveBudget(budget, effort)` now layers `defaults < effort preset < explicit /budget` at read time, so an explicit `/budget` cap wins over the preset regardless of command order, and `/budget` show routes through the same helper (inspectable). All call sites (loop, REPL banner + maxOutputTokens, `/budget` show) pass `effort`. NOTE confirmed via `bootstrap.ts:1433`: real `baseConfig.budget` is a partial (`{...config.toml[budget], ...CLI}`), never a full `DEFAULT_BUDGET`, so the middle layer is never shadowed (the test fixture's full-budget seed was unrealistic — tests now use a partial).
- **(8) Subagent effort inheritance (operator chose auto-inherit).** Provider-effort propagates parent→child so `/effort` applies task-wide; operational caps do NOT (stay per-playbook). New `HarnessConfig.providerEffort` (request-level reasoning axis, set directly) + `resolveProviderEffort(config)` (explicit `providerEffort` wins, else derive from `effort`) drive both the loop request and the spawn. Threaded through all 6 layers mirroring `--subagent-temperature`: `loop` (forward resolved value) → `runtime` input → `spawn-factory` `--subagent-effort` argv → `args` parse (allowlist-validated) → `index` args→opts → `subagent-child` opts→child config. The child sets `providerEffort` (not `effort`) so it reasons at the inherited depth with its own playbook caps.
- **(5) Gemini budget floor.** `effortThinkingBudget` now clamps BOTH ways — `MIN_THINKING_BUDGET = 128` (Gemini Pro min); below-floor headroom omits the block instead of sending a sub-minimum that 400s.
- **(10) `withRunningCue` extracted** to `slash/format.ts`; `/effort`, `/budget`, `/model` all use it (no more drifting copies).

Round-2 tests: `resolveProviderEffort` rule (effort.test.ts); `effectiveBudget` layering precedence + opt-out propagation (effort.test.ts); `/effort` records-level-only + order-independent precedence (commands.test.ts); Gemini below-floor → undefined (providers/effort.test.ts); `--subagent-effort` parse/allowlist (args.test.ts); parent→spawn-opts providerEffort forward (runtime.test.ts). Verification: `tests/harness` + `tests/subagents` + `tests/cli` **2884 pass / 0 fail**; `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Round 3 — persistence + footer + UX (operator-requested, 2026-06-02).**
- **Persistence via config.toml (NOT DB).** Operator asked whether to persist in the DB; recommended against — the DB is audit/session/derived state, config.toml is where operator defaults live (`[memory]`/`[providers]`/`[budget]`). Added `[effort] level` mirroring `[budget]`: new `loadEffortConfig` (user+project layers, fail-soft, unknown level warns, case-insensitive) in `config/loaders.ts`; bootstrap resolves `config.effort = loaded ?? DEFAULT_EFFORT` and surfaces `effortConfigWarnings`. `/effort` stays the in-session (in-memory) override. So precedence is `/effort` (session) > project `[effort].level` > user > `DEFAULT_EFFORT`. (A dedicated `[effort]` table, NOT `[sampling]` — the spec reserves `[sampling.*]` for per-provider sub-tables.)
- **Default effort = high.** `DEFAULT_EFFORT = 'high'` (`harness/effort.ts`), applied at bootstrap to the TOP-LEVEL session only (subagents still carry only inherited provider-effort, no operational-cap preset). Matches Anthropic's own default.
- **Footer chip.** `/effort`'s output dropped the redundant `provider effort: …` line. The selected level now shows in the footer right cluster (`effort: <level>`): seeded by `session:banner.effort` at boot, updated live by a new `effort:change` event the `/effort` command emits — mirrors how `operationMode` (banner + `mode:change`) works.

Round-3 tests: `loadEffortConfig` (empty/project/case-insensitive/unknown-warns/project-over-user) in loaders.test.ts; bootstrap `config.effort` defaults `high` in bootstrap.test.ts; reducer `effort:change` + `session:banner.effort` seed + `createInitialState` null in state.test.ts; footer renders/omits the chip in footer.test.ts; `/effort` no-longer-shows-provider-line in commands.test.ts; BootstrapResult fixtures updated for `effortConfigWarnings`. Verification: `tests/tui` + `tests/cli` **2766 pass / 0 fail** (+ config/bootstrap/state/footer suites green); `tsc --noEmit` + Biome clean.

**Round-3 review fixes (code-review, high — 2026-06-02).** Caught two real bugs in the round-3 diff: (a) the config key was `[sampling].effort`, which collides with the spec's reserved `[sampling.*]` namespace (`[sampling.thinking]` / `[sampling.openai_reasoning]`, where `effort` is OpenAI-specific) and would silently ignore spec-conformant config — renamed to a dedicated `[effort].level` table; (b) the config validated case-sensitively while `/effort` lowercases — made `[effort].level` case-insensitive to match. Tests updated + a case-insensitivity case added. Noted-not-changed (intended/by-design): default `high` applies the full operational profile (caps `maxToolErrors` 5→8, `maxConcurrentSubagents` 3→4; Gemini 2.5 now always sends a thinking budget) — this is the consequence of effort being a unified profile + the footer needing a concrete level to show. Open UI follow-ups: footer right-cluster overflows on 80-col (hard-truncates the model id; the effort chip worsens a pre-existing limitation); `/effort` updates the footer live but `/model` has no `model:change` event (chip stale) — asymmetry. Spec follow-up: document `[effort].level` in TOKEN_TUNING when reconciled. (Committed on `feat/effort`: `ac74c512` base feature, `73678fec` persistence+footer.)

**Effort inheritance — slash-playbook path (operator catch, 2026-06-02).** Round-2's (8) wired provider-effort inheritance on the harness loop's `task_*` spawn (`loop.ts`) but missed the OTHER spawn path: the REPL's `/<playbook>` dispatcher (`repl.ts` `runPlaybook`), which forwarded temperature/hooks to `runSubagent` but never `providerEffort`. So `/<playbook>` subagents ran at the provider default while the footer + `/effort` confirmation said a level was active. Fixed: `repl.ts` now forwards `resolveProviderEffort(baseConfig)` (provider axis only — operational caps stay per-playbook), mirroring the loop spawn site. Test: `slash playbook forwards the resolved provider-effort to runSubagent` (`tests/cli/repl.test.ts`). typecheck + Biome clean; repl suite 105 pass. Uncommitted (awaiting operator review).

**`/budget` mutation idempotency vs the effort preset (operator catch, 2026-06-02).** Round-3 routed `/budget` *show* through `effectiveBudget(budget, effort)`, but the *mutation* idempotency checks compared against `raw ?? DEFAULT_BUDGET`. So under `/effort low` (subagents preset 1, no explicit override) `/budget` showed `subagents: 1` yet `/budget subagents 3` matched the DEFAULT fallback (3), reported "already 3", and never wrote — the effective cap stayed 1, uneditable. Fixed (after a review of the fix itself): all cap mutations (`steps`, `parallel-tools`, `subagents`) now compare against the RAW explicit override (no `?? DEFAULT_BUDGET`, no effort-adjusted effective). `/budget` is the explicit-override surface, so a mutation always RECORDS (pins) the value — surviving later `/effort` changes (explicit beats preset in `effectiveBudget`) — and "already (no change)" fires only when the raw override is already that value. The first cut compared against `effectiveBudget(...)` instead, which fixed the original bug but introduced a silent non-pin (typing a value == the current preset said "already" and didn't stick across `/effort`); raw-compare avoids both. Tradeoff: `/budget`'s "already" now means "raw override already N" (differs from `/model`, which has no layer so raw == effective). `DEFAULT_BUDGET` dropped from `budget.ts` (now unused; `effectiveBudget` stays for *show*). Tests: override-beats-preset writes, pins-even-when-equal-to-preset, "already" only on a real raw repeat; existing idempotency tests unchanged (fixture has a full raw budget + no effort). typecheck + Biome clean; commands suite 84 pass. Uncommitted.

**Gemini legacy thinking-budget clamp (operator catch, 2026-06-02).** The Gemini adapter's legacy `thinking_budget` path forwarded the raw positive value (pre-existing behavior the effort refactor moved into `googleThinkingBudget`). But Gemini 2.5 caps `thinkingConfig.thinkingBudget` per model (Flash/Flash-Lite 24576, Pro 32768) and 400s above it, so a playbook with `sampling.thinking_budget: 50000` (the loader allows large values for provider-specific handling, without `sampling.max_tokens`) produced an API 400 instead of being fitted. Fixed: new `ProviderCapabilities.max_thinking_budget` (set per Gemini model), and `googleThinkingBudget` clamps the resolved budget (BOTH the effort-derived and legacy paths) to it via `Math.min`. Effort already topped at the 24576 ladder ceiling; clamping both keeps it correct if the ladder ever rises. Omitted cap ⇒ no clamp. Tests: legacy 50000→24576 (and effort 16384→8000 with a low cap), under-cap unchanged, no-cap=no-clamp (`google-effort.test.ts`). typecheck + Biome clean; `tests/providers` 203 pass. Uncommitted.

**REPL config-warning surfacing (operator catch, 2026-06-02).** `BootstrapResult` is consumed by BOTH entry points — `run.ts` (one-shot) and `repl.ts` (interactive) — and each surfaces config-loader warnings to stderr. `effortConfigWarnings` was added to `run.ts` only, so an invalid `[effort].level` in an interactive session silently fell back to `DEFAULT_EFFORT` with no diagnostic. Found a wider gap while there: `repl.ts` also never surfaced `providersConfigWarnings` or `budgetConfigWarnings` (pre-existing). Fixed: `repl.ts` now destructures and prints `providers` / `budget` / `effort` config warnings, matching `run.ts`'s block. Test: a REPL-boot test injects all three and asserts each is surfaced (the regression guard the gap lacked — the surfacing was previously untested in the REPL). Same class as the `/<playbook>` effort-forward miss: `repl.ts` is the parallel consumer easy to forget when patching `run.ts`/the loop. typecheck + Biome clean; `tests/cli/repl.test.ts` 106 pass. Uncommitted.

**Anthropic: decouple thinking from `effort` (operator catch, 2026-06-02).** Round-2 made `anthropicThinkingParam` engage adaptive thinking when `req.effort !== undefined`. After round-3 set the default effort to `high`, that turned extended thinking ON for essentially every Anthropic request. The break: Sonnet 4.6 is adaptive AND still accepts sampling (`supports_sampling` not false), but Anthropic rejects `temperature`/`top_p` modifications together with extended thinking on pre-4.7 models (HTTP 400). So every Sonnet-4.6 run with a sampling override — evals pinning `temperature: 0`, builtin verify-* subagents declaring `sampling.temperature: 0.1` — now 400'd before responding. (Opus 4.7/4.8 escape: `supports_sampling: false` strips the override; Haiku isn't adaptive so effort never engaged thinking there.) Fixed (the report's first option): thinking is engaged ONLY by an explicit `thinking_budget > 0`, never by `effort`. `effort` still rides `output_config.effort` (text/tool eagerness + thinking depth WHEN thinking is on) — which doesn't conflict with sampling — so deterministic runs stay valid. Reverses the round-2 "effort engages adaptive" call (it was wrong) and matches Anthropic's orthogonal model (effort works with OR without thinking). Gemini is unaffected (its only knob IS the thinking budget, and it has no sampling+thinking constraint). Tests: `effort alone → no thinking` (unit), and a Sonnet-4-6 integration test asserting effort+temperature emits `output_config.effort` + `temperature` with NO `thinking` block. typecheck + Biome clean; `tests/providers` 204 pass. Uncommitted.

**docs/BUDGET.md — budget operator guide (2026-06-02).** Wrote the English operational reference for the run-budget subsystem, matching the `docs/HOOKS.md` / `docs/MEMORY.md` "Operator Guide" house style (intro + spec-pointer to `AGENTIC_CLI §5` / `ORCHESTRATION §11`/§3.5.0, then numbered sections). Covers: the `RunBudget` model + "cost is the gate, steps the backstop" stance; every cap grouped (engagement gate / runaway backstops / concurrency / per-call shaping) with defaults + trigger + exit reason, grounded in `DEFAULT_BUDGET`; the `effectiveBudget` three-layer resolution (defaults < `/effort` preset < explicit) and order-independent precedence; the control-surface matrix (config.toml `[budget]`, `--max-steps`, `/effort` preset, `/budget`, playbook frontmatter) noting which caps each surface can/can't set; the `EXIT_REASONS` → status table; cost in depth (3 states, cumulative/resume-aware, soft cap); and subagent budgeting (per-playbook caps + pre-spawn cost projection). No code change.

## [2026-06-01] permissions — annotated/signed `git tag` is exec-capable (editor / gpg)

**Hole (operator review, follow-on to the git-hooks fix).** The git-hooks fix left `git tag` in the pure `git-write` group, but `git tag` invokes external CONFIGURABLE commands in two sub-modes: an annotated tag (`-a`) with NO `-m`/`-F` message opens `core.editor`, and a signed/verified tag (`-s`/`-u`/`-v`) runs `gpg.program`. Both are settable in a (cloned, attacker-controlled) repo's `.git/config`, so `git tag -a v1` or `git tag -s v1` is an arbitrary-local-exec surface — yet it was classified `git-write + read-fs`, which autonomous auto-approves as repo-confined (the pre-effect-classification low-confidence/default git path would have kept it behind a modal).

**Fix (`cmdGit` `case 'tag'` in `resolvers/bash.ts`).** Pulled `tag` out of the `add`/`stash`/`reset` group into a dedicated case that walks the flags: signed/verified (`-s`/`--sign`, `-u`/`--local-user`, `-v`/`--verify`) OR annotated (`-a`/`--annotate`) without a message (`-m`/`--message`, `-F`/`--file`) → `[exec('arbitrary'), gitWrite, readFs]`; everything else (lightweight `git tag v1`, `git tag -d`, annotated-WITH-message `-a -m msg`) stays `[gitWrite, readFs]`. Signing runs gpg even WITH a message, so `-s`/`-u`/`-v` gate regardless of `-m`. Short flags bundle (`-am`, `-as`, `-af`), so each bundle is walked left-to-right; `-m`/`-F`/`-u`/`-n` consume the rest of their token as an argument. `git -c …` is refused upstream, but a repo/user gitconfig `core.editor`/`gpg.program` still reaches these.

**Verification.** Scratch (14 cases): `-a v1`, `-s v1`, `-u KEY v1`, `-uKEY v1`, `-v v1`, `-as v1`, `-s v1 -m msg`, `-af v1` → exec:arbitrary; `-a v1 -m msg`, `-am msg v1`, `git tag v1`, `-d v1`, `-f v1`, `-l` → git-write, no exec. Engine autonomous (`allow: git*`): `git tag -a v1` / `git tag -s v1` → confirm; `git add -A && git tag v1` / `git tag -a v1 -m release` → allow.

**Tests.** `resolvers.test.ts`: an editor/gpg case (`-a`/`-s`/`-u`/`-v`/`-as`/`-s -m`/`-af` → exec:arbitrary) and a safe case (lightweight / `-d` / `-a -m` / `-am` → git-write, no exec); dropped `git tag` from the "no hook surface" group (it's now nuanced). `engine.test.ts`: an autonomous case pinning annotated/signed tag → confirm and lightweight / `-a -m` → allow. typecheck + Biome clean; `tests/permissions/` **2200 pass / 0 fail**. `docs/SECURITY.md` §3.2 — added the tag editor/gpg hatch to the git "escape hatches decoded" list, and back-filled the commit/merge/rebase/cherry-pick hooks hatch the git-hooks commit hadn't recorded there. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec drift (folds into the §287 git item already flagged).** `PERMISSION_ENGINE.md §287` (git resolver row) lists `commit`/`add`/`reset` → `git-write` with no mention of the commit-hooks exec (prior fix) or the tag editor/gpg exec (this fix); the pending §287 sync should cover both.

## [2026-06-01] permissions — gate ATTACHED awk include/load/exec flags (`-i/tmp/x`, `-lfoo`)

**Hole (operator review of the awk resolver).** GNU awk's external-program flags take a REQUIRED operand that can be ATTACHED: `awk -i/tmp/inc.awk …` (include + run a source file), `awk -lfoo …` (load/dlopen a shared library), `awk -E/tmp/prog.awk …` (exec). The `cmdAwk` predicate matched these flags by EXACT string (`AWK_EXTERNAL_FLAGS.has(t)`), so only the separate-operand form (`-i /tmp/inc.awk`) was caught; the attached form fell through as a side-effect-free awk program and emitted only `read-fs` — no `exec:arbitrary`. In any policy that allows `awk`, or any auto-approve path for repo-confined ops, that hid the real execution surface (the included source / loaded library runs arbitrary code).

**Fix (`cmdAwk` in `resolvers/bash.ts`).** Split `AWK_EXTERNAL_FLAGS` into `AWK_EXTERNAL_SHORT` (`-f`, `-i`, `-l`, `-E`, `-D`, `-p`) matched by PREFIX (catches both `-i x` and `-i/tmp/x`/`-lfoo`/`-Efile`) and `AWK_EXTERNAL_LONG` (`--file`/`--include`/`--load`/`--exec`/`--debug`/`--profile`) matched as `--flag` or `--flag=value`. The prefix match is case-sensitive so `-F` (field separator) and `-v` (assignment) do NOT false-match `-f`/`-i`/`-l`. This is a strict superset of the old detection and additionally closes the `--exec=`/`--debug=`/`--profile=` long-`=` forms the exact-match set missed.

**Verification.** Scratch: `awk -i/tmp/payload.awk …`, `awk -lfoo …`, `awk -Efile …`, `awk -Dcmds …`, `awk --include=… …`, `awk --exec=… …`, `awk -f prog.awk …` → exec:arbitrary; `awk '{print $1}' f.log`, `awk -F: '{print $1}' /etc/passwd`, `awk -v x=1 '{print x}'` → read-fs, NO exec (no over-gating; `-F`/`-v` not mistaken for external flags). All twelve cases matched the expected gate/no-gate.

**Tests.** `resolvers.test.ts`: an attached-external-flags case (`-i/tmp/x`, `-lfoo`, `-E/tmp/prog`, `--include=`, `--exec=` → exec:arbitrary) and a `-F`/`-v` no-false-positive pin. typecheck + Biome clean; `tests/permissions/` **2197 pass / 0 fail**. `docs/SECURITY.md` §3.2 awk row synced (now lists the external flags + attached/separate + case-sensitivity). No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec drift to sync (NOT yet edited — needs operator go-ahead).** `PERMISSION_ENGINE.md §291` (the awk effect row) lists the external flags (`-f`/`-i`/`--include`/`--load`/`--exec`/`--debug`/`--profile`) but does not note that the SHORT forms' operands may be attached. Should be updated to record the prefix (attached-or-separate) matching and the case-sensitivity carve-out for `-F`/`-v`.

## [2026-06-01] permissions — recognize BUNDLED sed -i suffixes (`-Ei.bak`) as in-place writes

**Hole (operator review of the sed resolver, follow-on to the BSD-suffix fix).** `sed`'s `-i` short flag can be BUNDLED after other short flags with an attached suffix: `sed -Ei.bak 's/x/y/' /etc/hosts` (= `-E` extended-regex + `-i.bak` in-place) is a valid GNU invocation that edits the file in place. The in-place detection only treated tokens that START with `-i...` (or `--in-place...`) as in-place, so `-Ei.bak` fell through and the command was modeled as a read-only stdout transform — emitting only `read-fs:/etc/hosts`. Because the §11 protected-path floor escalates `/etc` for WRITE ops only (reads pass), the protected write was hidden from BOTH gating (bypass mode allowed it silently) AND the audit (recorded as a read, not a write).

**Fix (`cmdSed` in `resolvers/bash.ts`).** Replaced the prefix-match in-place detection (and the ad-hoc separate-operand regex from the prior fix) with one `sedInPlaceForm(tokens)` walk that classifies the `-i` flag wherever it appears in a short bundle: `none` / `attached` (suffix is part of the token — `-i.bak`, `-Ei.bak`, `-niE`, or GNU `--in-place` — script at positional[0], modeled as a write) / `separate` (a bare `-i` at the token end — `-i`, `-ni`, `-Ei` — BSD consumes the next token as the suffix, script-position-ambiguous). The walk stops at `-e`/`-f`, which consume the rest of the token as their argument, so an `i` inside a script/file arg (`-ne`, `-ei`) is NOT mistaken for the in-place flag. `separate` + no `-e` → exec:arbitrary (the prior fix); `attached` (or `separate` with `-e`) → `write-fs(operands)`.

**Verification.** Scratch: `sed -Ei.bak 's/x/y/' /etc/hosts` → `write-fs:/etc/hosts` (was read-fs only); `sed -Ei.bak … file`, `sed -niE … file` → write-fs(operands), no exec; `sed -Ei … file` (bare i, no -e) → exec:arbitrary; `sed -ne 's/x/p/' f` → read-fs, NO write (the `-e` arg-consumer guard — no false positive); `sed -i.bak`/`-i -e`/`--in-place` unchanged. Engine bypass mode: `sed -Ei.bak 's/x/y/' /etc/hosts` → `confirm` (the §11 floor now sees the write); the in-repo operand → `allow`.

**Tests.** `resolvers.test.ts`: a bundled-in-place case (`-Ei.bak … /etc/hosts` → write-fs:/etc/hosts; `-Ei.bak`/`-niE` → write-fs, no exec) and a `-ne`-is-not-in-place no-false-positive pin. `engine.test.ts`: a bypass-mode case asserting `sed -Ei.bak 's/x/y/' /etc/hosts` escalates to `confirm` (colocated with the `write_file /etc/hosts` §11-floor test). typecheck + Biome clean; `tests/permissions/` **2195 pass / 0 fail**. `docs/SECURITY.md` §3.2 sed row already described attached-suffix → write-fs; extended the example to call out the bundled forms. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec drift (folds into the §292 item already flagged below).** `PERMISSION_ENGINE.md §292` (the sed effect row) says `-i`/`--in-place` read-only script → `write-fs(operands)` with no mention of bundled `-i` detection or the BSD separate-suffix split; the pending §292 sync should cover both this and the prior separate-suffix fix.

## [2026-06-01] permissions — sed -i BSD separate-suffix ambiguity: bare `-i` without `-e` → exec:arbitrary

**Hole (operator review of the sed resolver).** On BSD/macOS, `sed -i` takes the backup suffix as a SEPARATE operand (`-i extension`), consuming the NEXT token — ANY token, not just `''` or `.bak`. That shifts the script one position right of where GNU puts it. `sed -i p 's/x/id/e' file`: GNU reads `{script: 'p', files: ['s/x/id/e','file']}`, but BSD reads `{suffix: 'p', script: 's/x/id/e', file: 'file'}` — and the BSD script execs `id` via the `s///e` flag. The resolver took `positional[0]` (`p`, a read-only print script) as the script and modeled read-only + in-repo writes → auto-approvable under autonomous, even though the real BSD script runs arbitrary code. The prior partial guard only fired when the token after `-i` was `''` or `.`-prefixed (`-i .bak`), so a normal-looking suffix like `p` slipped through.

**Fix (`cmdSed` in `resolvers/bash.ts`).** When `-i` is present in its separate-operand form (a bare `-i` token, or a short-flag bundle ending in `i` like `-ni`, matched by `/^-[A-Za-z]*i$/`) AND there is no `-e`/`--expression`, the script position is platform-divergent → fail-closed to `exec:arbitrary`. The command still rides `write-fs` on EVERY positional operand so the bypass §11 protected-path floor keeps catching an escalate/deny target (`sed -i s/a/b/ /etc/hosts` → `write-fs:/etc/hosts`) that exec:arbitrary alone wouldn't surface to a path-based floor; over-attributing a write to a script/suffix token is harmless (repo-confined).

**Exempt (script position unambiguous → stay modeled, auto-approvable):** `-e`/`--expression` (the script rides the flag value, which coincides with the real script on BOTH platforms — even though BSD eats the `-e` token as the suffix, the next token then becomes `positional[0]` == the extracted `-e` value); an ATTACHED suffix (`-i.bak`, `-in` — the suffix is part of the `-i` token, so `positional[0]` is the script on both); and the GNU-only long form `--in-place[=SUFFIX]` (no BSD counterpart that shifts). The deny-tier refuse (`sed -i /proc/sysrq-trigger`) is unaffected — it fires in `analyzeCommand`'s per-arg classifier upstream of the handler.

**Why not honor `-e` only when the suffix "looks like" a suffix.** The prior `''`/`.bak` heuristic assumed a BSD suffix is empty or dot-prefixed. BSD's `-i extension` accepts any string, so the heuristic was unsound. The robust signal is structural: a separate-operand `-i` shifts the script regardless of what the suffix looks like.

**Verification.** Scratch: `sed -i p 's/x/id/e' file`, `sed -i 's/a/b/' file`, `sed -i bak 's/a/b/e' file`, `sed -ni p …`, `sed -i '' …` → exec:arbitrary; `sed -i.bak …`, `sed -i -e …`, `sed --in-place …` → write-fs(operands), no exec; `sed 's/a/b/' file` (no -i) → read-fs. `sed -i s/a/b/ /etc/hosts` still emits write-fs:/etc/hosts (floor intact); `sed -i /proc/sysrq-trigger` still Refuses. Engine autonomous with `allow: sed*`: `sed -i p 's/x/id/e' file` → not allow.

**Tests.** `resolvers.test.ts`: replaced the now-inaccurate "GNU -i forms resolve the script" case (bare `-i` is no longer cleanly resolved) with a hole case (`-i p`/`-i 's/a/b/'`/`-i bak`/`-ni p` → exec:arbitrary) and an unambiguous-forms case (`-i.bak`/`-i -e`/`--in-place` → write-fs, no exec); retargeted the "sed -i → write-fs(operands)" case to the unambiguous `-i.bak`. The existing `-i ''`/`-i .bak` → exec:arbitrary case and the `/etc/hosts` floor + `/proc` refuse cases still pass unchanged. `engine.test.ts`: a new autonomous case pinning `sed -i p 's/x/id/e' file` → not auto-approved under `allow: sed*`. typecheck + Biome clean; `tests/permissions/` **2192 pass / 0 fail**. `docs/SECURITY.md` §3.2 sed row synced. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec drift to sync (NOT yet edited — needs operator go-ahead, per the no-spec-edit-without-request rule).** `PERMISSION_ENGINE.md §292` (the sed effect row) describes `-i`/`--in-place` read-only script → `write-fs(operands)` with NO mention of the BSD separate-suffix ambiguity (it predates even the prior `''`/`.bak` partial guard). Should be updated to record that a bare `-i` without `-e` is script-position-ambiguous → exec:arbitrary, while `-i.bak`/`-i -e`/`--in-place` stay modeled.

## [2026-06-01] permissions — git commit/merge/rebase/cherry-pick run repo hooks → exec:arbitrary (keep the modal)

**Hole (operator review of the autonomous capability-confinement).** A compound like `git add -A && git commit -m wip` auto-approved under autonomous because the resolver classified `commit` as `git-write` (repo-confined) — but `git commit` is NOT just a local metadata write. By default it runs repository hooks under `.git/hooks/` (or `core.hooksPath`): `pre-commit`, `prepare-commit-msg`, `commit-msg`, `post-commit`. A repo that ships an installed hook (a cloned project, a dependency checkout) executes that arbitrary code on a bare `git commit` — with no prompt. Same class for `merge` (pre-merge-commit / commit-msg / post-merge), `rebase` (pre-rebase / post-rewrite, and `--exec <cmd>` runs `<cmd>` verbatim), and `cherry-pick` (the commit hooks per replayed commit). All four were in the auto-approving `git-write` group.

**Fix (resolver layer, `cmdGit` in `resolvers/bash.ts`).** Split the four hook-running verbs out of the `git-write` group into `[exec('arbitrary'), gitWrite(REPO), readFs(REPO)]`. `exec:arbitrary` is never repo-confined, so the autonomous gate keeps the modal; the `git-write` + `read-fs` caps still ride along so the engine's §11 floors stay honest about the metadata write. `add` / `tag` / `stash` / `reset` stay pure `git-write` (no pre-add/tag/reset hook; `git stash` writes via `commit-tree`/`update-ref` plumbing that bypasses commit hooks) → still auto-approvable. `push`/`pull`/`fetch` were already gated by `net-egress`; `git -c`/`--git-dir`/`--work-tree`/`--exec-path` are already global Refuses.

**`--no-verify` is NOT a safe downgrade.** The operator's report offered "exec:arbitrary unless `--no-verify`/hooks proven absent". But `--no-verify` (`-n`) bypasses ONLY `pre-commit` and `commit-msg` — `prepare-commit-msg` and `post-commit` still run, so a `post-commit` hook fires under `git commit --no-verify` and executes arbitrary code. Honoring `--no-verify` would re-open the hole, so the fix does NOT downgrade on it (verified: `git commit --no-verify` still emits exec:arbitrary). The only robust "hooks absent" signal would be `git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null …`, which is already Refused upstream (the `-c` guard), so there is no auto-approve path to model.

**Verification.** Scratch: `git commit -m x`, `git commit --no-verify -m x`, `git merge`, `git rebase`, `git cherry-pick` → exec:arbitrary; `git add`/`tag`/`stash`/`reset` → git-write only. Engine under autonomous: with `allow: git*`, all four hook-runners → `confirm` (the capability-confinement withholds auto-approval even though policy permits git), while `git add -A && git tag wip` → `allow`; without an allow rule the single forms hit strict default-deny (`deny`) — either way NOT auto-approved.

**Tests.** `engine.test.ts`: the pre-existing `local git-write auto-approves` case switched its fixture `git add -A && git commit` → `git add -A && git tag wip` (commit is no longer a pure git-write), plus a new case asserting commit/merge/rebase/cherry-pick (incl. `--no-verify`) stay `confirm` under `allow: git*`. `resolvers.test.ts`: a case pinning the four verbs → exec:arbitrary (+git-write rides along) and a control pinning add/tag/stash/reset → git-write, no exec:arbitrary. The score-gate fixtures that use `git commit` still hold (capability_risk is max-based — exec:arbitrary and git-write are both critical, so the score stays 0.40). typecheck + Biome clean; `tests/permissions/` **2190 pass / 0 fail**. No commit (awaiting operator review).

**Spec drift to sync (NOT yet edited — needs operator go-ahead, per the no-spec-edit-without-request rule).** `PERMISSION_ENGINE.md §287` (git resolver row) still lists `commit` under `→ git-write(repo)` and the "Escape hatches decodados" sentence omits the commit-hooks case; `AGENTIC_CLI.md §8.1` (the "Repo-confinada" definition) cites "`git-write` local" auto-approving (still true for add/tag/stash/reset, but commit/merge/rebase/cherry-pick now fall under the "sempre confirma — exec:arbitrary" clause); `PERMISSION_ENGINE.md §113` uses `commit` as a `git-write` example. Should be synced so the spec records that hook-running verbs are exec:arbitrary.

## [2026-06-01] permissions — cwd-scope symlink escape: route to Conservative so autonomous keeps the modal

**Hole (operator review of the autonomous capability-confinement).** The engine's repo-confined predicate (`capRepoConfined`) keys on a LEXICAL `startsWithSegment(path, cwd)`, so a `read-fs:<cwd>/link` / `write-fs:<cwd>/link` cap reads as repo-confined even when `link` is a symlink whose realpath escapes the cwd. The bash resolver DOES detect that escape (`detectCwdScopeEscape`, realpath-based), but the slice-178 defense only **degraded confidence to `low`** — and `ok/low` is exactly what a `**`-glob read (`wc -l src/**/*.ts`) also resolves to, which the new autonomous posture deliberately auto-approves. So `cat link && echo ok` (where `<cwd>/link → /tmp/secret`) was downgraded to `ok/low`, the lexical `read-fs:<cwd>/link` cap passed `capRepoConfined`, and `autoApproveRepoConfined` cleared the modal — reading outside the repo without the confirm the low-confidence path was meant to force. The realpath escape signal was being discarded at the engine layer because it was conflated with benign glob-low at the `kind: ok` boundary.

**Fix (resolver layer, `resolvers/bash.ts`).** A cwd-scope escape now routes to `kind: conservative` — NOT merely low confidence — via a `cwdEscaped` flag tracked SEPARATELY from the escalate-tier `escalated` flag.
